{"step_by_step_thinking": "The question asks whether a dedicated discharge coordinator improves the quality of hospital discharge. The retrieved documents from MEDCORP emphasize the importance of comprehensive, interprofessional discharge planning to improve patient outcomes, reduce readmissions, and ensure continuity of care. Document [11] specifically references a Care Transitions Intervention (CTI) program where older patients are paired with a discharge nurse transition coach. This coach provides support before discharge, at home, and via follow-up calls, which leads to a significant reduction in 30- and 90-day readmission rates. Document [12] describes a multidisciplinary team that includes a nurse for discharge planning, a pharmacist for follow-up, and a discharge instruction booklet, resulting in a 31% post-discharge hospital utilization rate in the intervention group versus 44% in the control group. These results indicate that having a dedicated coordinator—such as a discharge nurse or transition coach—improves patient outcomes by enhancing communication, ensuring medication reconciliation, and promoting adherence to care plans. The evidence consistently shows that such coordination leads to better patient education, reduced readmissions, and improved quality of care. Therefore, the presence of a dedicated discharge coordinator directly contributes to improved quality of hospital discharge.", "answer_choice": "A"}
